description.task: Simulate Spring Boot's auto-configuration mechanism in plain Java. Create a configuration detection system that automatically applies configurations based on what's 'on the classpath'.
instructions: In configure(), always add 'CoreAutoConfiguration' -> 'Configured Spring core context'.
If 'spring-web' is in availableLibraries, add 'WebMvcAutoConfiguration' -> 'Configured DispatcherServlet on port 8080'.
If 'spring-security' is present, add 'SecurityAutoConfiguration' -> 'Configured Spring Security filters'.
description.hint: Spring Boot uses @ConditionalOnClass to apply configurations only when certain classes are present. This is what makes it 'convention over configuration'.
description.expectedOutput:
Applied configurations:
  CoreAutoConfiguration -> Configured Spring core context
  WebMvcAutoConfiguration -> Configured DispatcherServlet on port 8080
  JpaAutoConfiguration -> Configured EntityManagerFactory
  SecurityAutoConfiguration -> Configured Spring Security filters

description.task: Demonstrate the principle of Convention over Configuration by building a smart URL router that uses method naming conventions to automatically determine routes.
instructions: In discoverRoutes(), iterate all public methods. For methods starting with 'get', 'post', or 'delete', create a route key 'METHOD /path' where path is the rest of the name lowercased.
Ignore methods not starting with those prefixes.
In handle(), find the route 'METHOD /path' and invoke the method on controller, then print the result.
description.hint: Spring MVC's @GetMapping, @PostMapping follow similar conventions. Spring Boot auto-detects @Controller classes in the package without configuration.
description.expectedOutput:
Route: GET /users -> getUsers
Route: POST /user -> postUser
Route: DELETE /user -> deleteUser
[User list]

description.task: Model and print the typical directory structure of a Spring Boot application. Then write code that validates whether a given structure follows Spring Boot conventions.
description.context: Understanding the standard Spring Boot project structure helps you navigate and organize large applications effectively.
instructions: Implement getMissingPaths() to filter required paths not in projectPaths.
In Main, print 'Complete structure valid: [true/false]' and 'Incomplete structure valid: [true/false]'.
For the incomplete structure, print 'Missing: [list of missing paths]'.
description.hint: Spring Boot follows Maven Standard Directory Layout. The main class annotated with @SpringBootApplication should be at the root of your base package.
description.expectedOutput:
Complete structure valid: true
Incomplete structure valid: false
Missing: [src/test/java, pom.xml]
